questions.q: What is the average-case complexity of hash table lookup?
Why are hash tables fast in the average case?
questions.a: A good hash spreads keys uniformly so bucket access stays close to constant time.

questions.q: When can a hash table degrade toward O(n)?
questions.a: When many keys collide because of a poor hash function, a high load factor, or adversarial input.

questions.q: What two properties should a good hash function have?

questions.a: Small key changes should flip many output bits so nearby keys do not cluster.

questions.q: Why is a predictable hash a security risk?
questions.a: An attacker can craft colliding keys and force worst-case behavior.

questions.q: What is the load factor of a hash table?
Why does a higher load factor hurt performance?
questions.a: More keys share the same table, so collisions and probe lengths increase.

questions.q: Why do implementations resize instead of letting the table fill up?
questions.a: Resizing keeps lookups and inserts fast, and the one-time O(n) cost is amortized over many operations.

questions.q: What collision strategy stores a list per bucket?
Why can chaining be easier to reason about than open addressing?
questions.a: Each bucket can grow independently, so deletion and growth are simpler.

questions.q: When is chaining a better choice than open addressing?
questions.a: When deletions are frequent or the table needs to tolerate very high load factors.

questions.q: What collision strategy probes other slots in the array?

questions.a: The entire table lives in one array, so probes touch nearby memory locations.

questions.q: When does open addressing become a poor fit?
questions.a: When the table gets crowded and probe sequences get long or deletions are frequent.

questions.q: What problem does consistent hashing solve?
questions.a: Minimizing key movement when nodes change.

questions.q: Why is consistent hashing useful in distributed caches?
questions.a: Adding or removing a node only remaps a small fraction of keys.

questions.q: When would you prefer consistent hashing over regular modulo partitioning?
questions.a: When backend membership changes often and you want stable key placement.
